speaker: interviewer
text: This is the beginning of tape number two, an interview with Edith Cimmer. OK, when we left off, you were talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: They took advantage of the girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, exactly. What I would like to ask you, how did your friends-- your Ukrainian and your Polish friends-- treat you differently? Did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: When Hitler came in, they were still-- they were still nice, except the young-- you know, the young-- the boys that became like-- like they brainwashed them. And they became like they had to be bullies. And they see the Polish police, Ukrainian police. Not Jewish. Jewish was not allowed. And they used to have meetings like once a week. And they had to call them to them Heil Hitler. And they had to listen whatever they-- they told them. You know, the German. Whatever, they had to listen for them. So they became already very mean and very brutal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything ever happen to you, brutal?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, no. Not from the beginning. But they took advantage. Like they took the girls like me, 15, 14 years old. And the streets were not paved. And they took the bikes, and they drove through the street with the bikes like about six, seven men. And they came back and we had to take-- and it was a lot of dirt, a lot of-- it was raining-- and we had to clean the spikes from the bikes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every spike had to shine, completely. And whoever didn't do it-- so they-- they had-- like you hit the horses. How you call the-- a [POLISH]. How do you say in English? Like a rein or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they used to hit them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, a whip.

speaker: interviewee
text: Used to whip them. And then the bikes were spotless so they went again on this street all the way, like a whole day like this. And to again came full of dirt and everything, and we had to wash the bikes. Back and forth and back and forth. That's the way they used to take advantage of us. And then one day, we had to wash the floor in the police station. Was a big social hall and we washed the floor. And a girlfriend of mine, she was standing by the window. And he took with a gun and he shot her straight in the head. And her meat in the head and everything was on the-- on the windowsills, on the wall, on the floor, all over the pieces. He shot her right away. And he said, [NON-ENGLISH]. That means, damn it, I shot at her. I turned right. That-- you know, he did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was happy that he did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That mean, [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means, I killed her. That he was timing, and it was OK. And then we had to-- all over, the hand, the floor, the walls was all over, you know with-- full of blood, and full of meat. The eyes and the head cut in half all over. And we had to clean, us girls. We had to clean. We started screaming and shouting and crying, terrible. We became very emotional. And her parents came over. They called right away the parents. And it was hysterical. And they said, if you're not going to shut your mouth, you're going to be killed right away, all of you. We'll kill you. And then within a week later, I went again to wash the floor, to work over there and to-- to wash the floor. We had to carry the pails with water from far away from the well and we had some brooms. And it's not like here that you have carpeting, you have a sweeper. You had to wash with your-- with your-- on the knees with a-- with a pail with water. And to scrub with a broom. And then he tried to kill me, to put the bullet towards me. And instead, I didn't even realize. So I stood by the window so another boy, not the same. And instead, the bullet to go into me-- I didn't even realize. I was just standing to wipe out-- the blood was still on the second day, was still dried off from the day that they killed her. And he didn't get me. The bullet went into the wall, and I was alive. So he was kind of upset. [NON-ENGLISH]. That means, next time he'll-- he'll do it a better job. Like he didn't pull the-- you know, did straight to me. And they took a lot of advantages. Finally, it came one day and they gave us notice. And that's the way it went like a whole year, while we were still in the homes. We had to go in ghetto. They took like two-- two streets, and they made with barricades with the wires, like a fence, real tall fence. It's like in a jail, you know. Then-- in what in the jail for them was better. Like the day in jail, it's a lot if-- because they have-- I see on television. I've never been in jail, but I see on television-- they have a lot of land, and they walk around outside. They doing exercise and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took like two big blocks and they barricade it. And the men separate, the woman separate, and the young girls separate, the middle age separate. And they pulled the ladies and we used to sleep in like a room like 25, 30 girls. We slept on the floor. We just had straw on the floor. Were you with your mother and your sisters at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went to the ghetto, so we went all together. You're not allowed to take anything except whatever you can put on on you, like another-- I took another slip, and another slip, and another dress. How much can you carry? And this is it, nothing else. Just like that. And when we came to the ghetto, they took away my mother from there, from us-- separate. She was 44 years old. She was very young. And I was with the sisters together in the room. When we got up and just to go outside-- and excuse me, my expression-- we had so much lice on our body. The skin was full of like little holes. So we used to go outside and just, with the hand, just like that, shovel from our body the lice. That was on the straw. They never changed the straw. And they took us. So I didn't see my mother anymore since-- since the day beginning of 1942. We didn't see her. Just the three sisters. We were separated. Because the middle-- so we don't know whether they killed her before or whether they killed her while the last day from the slaughter. We couldn't see her, whether she was in a different block, different house, because they separated us completely. Different-- the elderly people and us, they used to take. We walked like two, three miles. And the German and the Polish soldiers, they took us with the rifles and with guns and we went on the field and we worked. I digged like the coal-- you know. Three layers from the ground, I digged the coal, you know, to this and pour it on a wheelbarrow. And I had to carry it real heavy all the way on the empty lot. It should dry out. So whatever the girls, whatever-- and the young men, too, but the girls were separated. And the men, we saw them from a distance. Whatever some of them, they couldn't carry anymore, so they took right away a gun and with a rifle, and they shot them. They didn't even-- they couldn't walk or anything. And then they picked up about five, six Jewish strong men, that they were still strong enough. And they took those people on a wheelbarrow and they carried away. They grabbed over some with a shovel, and they-- I don't even know where they buried them, you know, because they were dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to work like that every day and we came from work. So we had to stay in the line for a little bit of soup. The soup were without salt. That time was an epidemic for salt. So they gave us some-- a little bit of soup. Stood in the line. And they gave us a little piece, a small little piece of bread. And that's all. That's all we could eat. All day, that's all you were given to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So we didn't have any-- we didn't have enough food. We were starving and starvation. We were hungry. And then I-- I was afraid they shouldn't kill my sister. They starting to get already very weak. So one farmer came, and that was almost on the end of this year.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one farmer came over with a full wagon with hay. And I thought to myself, I'm going to run away in a village, like in a farm. And I hid on the wagon under the hay. They didn't see me, and I ran away to a farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him-- that time, there was still-- the farmers were still good to the Jews. They didn't kill them. And I said, I want to work by you. And every time they went, once a week it was like a bazaar, a market, a big market with the horses. They went to the city. And I used to work there. And they used to-- and he used to give me pieces of bread and everything. And I told him in what barrack my sisters are through the fence. And he came over. He said like a lie-- he come just to say hello, and this. They were yet allowed to see. And they gave my sisters a piece of bread that the Germans shouldn't see. And I helped them, too. So I didn't see my sisters already until-- until they got a notice that all the Jews around the city, like-- like around the state of Berezno, the big city like Michigan, Detroit, the main city over there, like was Berezno. And those two days, it's going to be they should get prepared all from the ghettos. They're going to be the slaughter-- the killing, already.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't tell, did they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they didn't tell the Jewish people, but they had the meeting-- the-- the soldiers from all around the city. I mean, the police and the Germans are coming. And they taking-- they took already like four or six men, the healthy one, on the-- on the lot. And they dig those three graves, the three graves that I have the picture over here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they're going to kill. So the farmer came-- I was there on the farm. So he came and he says to me, you know what? I'm sorry. I like you very much. You're a wonderful worker, you're a good worker and everything. And while I was-- see here, still you see the sign? A scar.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- how you call it. A [POLISH]. You know what that mean? That you-- that you take the corn and like it was in-- you know, in September already--

speaker: interviewer
text: At the harvest.

speaker: interviewee
text: The harvest. So with this, I cut off all the way. They were standing and looking how I'm doing so-- all the way. And I cut off this. This was hanging, the whole piece. So you see? See the marks over here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working. So then he says, I'm sorry. I have to take you to the city because today they're killing, or tomorrow morning, they're killing already all the Jew. It's going to be the slaughter from the ghettos. That's already the Germans made the-- the note. You know? I'm sorry. Now, how long were you or your sisters in the ghetto? How many months? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the ghetto almost a year. We were a year in the homes, and a year they took us in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is toward the end, fall of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of '42. So while-- well, I have no choice. So I was on the wagon and they were like a lot of, you know, people. were-- they were-- some of them were a shoemaker in the country, in the village. Some of them was a-- a tailor. Some of them were a farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of few Jewish were out of the ghetto and working there. So he says to me, the farmer-- he was a very nice fellow-- he says to me, you know what, [NON-ENGLISH]? He spoke Ukrainish to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I'm in the front. I'm holding the horses. And you are in the back of the wagon. They're going to kill you. Run away. Jump off from the wagon and run in the forest, because they're going to-- they're going to kill you anyways. Maybe you'll survive. And-- and-- I-- I'm going to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll say I'm watching the horses and I didn't see. I didn't watch in the back, you know, in the-- on the wagon. And I thought to myself, where should I run? Where should I go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't realize that much, what they're going to do. I didn't. I was 15 years old. And I had my mind like a 10 years old. I was very immature because at home, I was the youngest. And we were very-- when my sisters was reading on Shabbat, mostly, on Saturday, and they went to the library and they read the books or something, if they read about-- about more like men and love and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they didn't let me to know, to read. So I was immature. I didn't know anything what means love, or what means sex, or what means this. I didn't know yet. In Europe, the life, it completely was different than here in United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: As kids, we were very-- in an orthodox home, we were very polite. We were very nice. And we didn't know the kind of business with boys like here in United States. I was very immature.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so then I thought to myself, where I'm going-- where I'm going to run? There's nowhere. I'll run in the forest, they'll kill me. I'd rather be with my sisters together-- let them kill me with my sisters. So they brought me in. This, I believe in a miracle. Because there's so many nice Jewish girls and Jewish boys-- they were beauties. They were so talented, and educated, and good looking-- the Jewish, you know, generation was so smart. And girls were beauties. They could have been Miss America and everything, good looking. What am I? I'm nothing. So they brought me in, and first they took to the police station. So they took already all the people from the ghettos. It was a huge-- a huge building in the police station. And the screaming and the shouting was one and the other. The seventh heaven. They were already dead before one was stepping on the other, because it was like it says, but 2,000, 3,000 people in such a room, small. So there was a door. And they brought in-- so one policeman stays on one side of the door, and the other stays on the other side. And they bring the people and they check in their mouths if they still having gold, or maybe a ring or something. So they have with-- with pliers, and they pulled the tooth from the mouth with the pliers. And the gold, or watches, or whatever they had-- so they put it on a side. And I walked in-- before me, what I saw with my own eyes, and I walk in and he says, oh, [NON-ENGLISH]. He says, she's such a cute Jewish girl. Why to kill her? And I looked at them. Why me? Am I the only one? There's so many nice Jewish girls. And I think, why me, [NON-ENGLISH]? So he says-- and they didn't-- they look in my mouth, I didn't-- I was young. I didn't have any gold anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't pull me in in that room. So they say, go in the other room and hide yourself. And after the slaughter is going to be over, then-- then you're going to go out. And they didn't push me in in the room where the thousands of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: This, I believe in a miracle, because why me? I went in the other room. So what do I see in that room? I see pillows, I see quilts, I see some wares, I see Jewish goods, coats, and everything. That after the slaughter is going to be over, they're going to take this home to their wives. So I thought to myself, where should I hide? I hide under the quilt, and they're going to come and they're going to rape me, one after another. They'll take advantage. They're going to cut the arm. They're going to cut the ear.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they're going to take maybe the same what I saw what they did to the rabbi, how they punished him. They cut him to pieces, and the screaming with the street. And they're going to do this to me. And they're going to rape me and take advantage. No. There's no place here to hide. I'd rather go and let them better kill me with this. And I didn't see my sisters anymore, since then. And my mother-- I didn't know whether they killed her that day, or whether they killed her before because they separated my mother. And I walked out. And that was on the first floor. That was on the first floor. So I walked with the steps. I didn't know, because I'd never been at the police station. So I walked in in the room, and I opened the door, and I walk in the room where all the Germans and the Ukrainian and everything, they're waiting already to go on the field to kill. And they start to-- and I was standing like that. And they start to [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means, Ukrainish, that a Jewish girl ran away. So they took me back and put it in-- in-- in the room. They put in the room.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they separated. The elderly, they killed right away. First the elderly, like the 70, the 80, and before, they killed them. They used to take the knives, and they cut their ears, and they cut their eyes to take out the life, like I mentioned before. So why they took them to the grave, so they used to take advantage. And then the screaming. And then they took the middle class. And then they took the young, like me. We were 10-- 10 and 10, 20 couples.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they lay-- so I was in between them. And they took me on the field. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it from the police station?

speaker: interviewee
text: To walk? Maybe a half a mile from the police station to the field. And there, I saw already those three graves, the big graves. And I stayed. And then they brought the children. And I see, like, they take the small little kids with the [NON-ENGLISH], to tore apart in a half, and they throw in the graves, small babies. The screaming, the shouting-- you have no idea. And then 10 people had to lay one on the other, on one side grave, and 10 on the other side of the grave. And they picked up the stronger Jewish men. And they-- they-- they shot so one in the head, one in the arm, one in the leg.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were still alive. And the man had to stay with the shovels, and grab them, put them in the grave. And they were still alive. The grave became so tall, like-- like a mountain, you know? Because the people were still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And while we were there, they came out. So first while we were walking to the grave and we see the screaming and the shouting, then I realize what they going to do. I didn't realize that much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought to myself, why didn't I ran away? Why didn't I hid? I should ran away. So while we were starting to lay, so it was-- the plot was all barricaded with wires, you know, high wires. And the bottom, like the window, this was a big hole.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who did it. Maybe a big hole that you can-- real, real big hole. And one policeman, some of them, even, the police or the men, they couldn't take the-- the-- you know, the screaming. And then I wore yet-- I had a shawl, a big shawl. It was already cool.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wore the shawl. And then he says, the man-- and he was such a nice man before, the Ukrainian man. He says to me, while I was on the wagon, he says to me, you know-- so here, he's feeling sorry for me. You know what? Where they're taking you? I think they're taking you and the sisters and everybody, all the Jew, every Jew in this, they're going to wipe out everything. And then so he's feeling sorry for me. And then he says, you know what? Give me your shawl. The same man. Give me your shawl. They're killing you anyway. So why do you need the shawl? Let me have for my wife to home-- you know, Europe, they wore such a big wool shawl. And I thought to myself, look at that. He's so nice. Here he's feel sorry for me, that why to kill such a healthy and everything innocent people. Why they different? And here, he's want me to give him-- to give him the shawl instead somebody else will take away. Let me have it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to him, no. I can't give it to you. Until they'll kill me, I'm cold, and I have to wear it. So I didn't gave it to him. So while we were-- we came to the grave--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was doing the shooting at the grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was doing the shooting? One I-- the Polish boys and Ukrainian, and one German. In one hand, he was holding an apple eating, and in one hand, like a sub-machine, a-- a rifle that's short. And he was shooting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- and I see and I thought to myself, God, where are you? Why don't you show any miracle? Why? Why? Should be like a-- a hurricane or tornado or something, and to kill them. Because the screaming and the shouting, it was unbearable, unbearable the screaming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially the babies. The way, you know, throw them like a piece of sand, a piece of rock. And all of them, they were surrounded in the hundreds. So one says to me, to us, he says, he couldn't-- some had a heart and they couldn't take it. I don't know. I don't know, it's a miracle. I don't understand myself what happened, if meant to live, or what. And he says, run away. Don't lay. Run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just like that. He looks, run. Who said that to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A policeman. Not a German, but a policeman. But I don't know whether he was Ukrainian or whether he was Polish. Just run. And we start to run before we lay.

speaker: interviewee
text: It came our line already, because it was one after another, one after another, 10 couples, and 10 couples, and 10 couples, you know? They shot, and then it's another going. And the policeman going after us with-- with ammunition. So we start to run. And they start to shoot after us. So I ran from this. And I didn't look around. I didn't know. I just ran because the field was not far on a side, there were forest. And I ran. Whom the-- the bullet got, how many got killed, how many ran? I don't know whether they all got killed, or I'm the only one. I didn't look around. And I ran deep in the forest. And I climbed right away on the-- on the tree. OK. So let me ask you this for clarification. So basically what was happening, so you would have to go, lay down in the grave--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they would come and shoot you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they were standing like from the couch from there over here, and they were just like, like I say, I saw the German. In one hand, he had-- that was in the morning. They start already about 7, 8 o'clock in the morning to take from the-- because there were like 3,000 people. First they took from the ghettos and the-- and the police. And then we had to walk to the field, to the graves. So he was holding an apple in one hand, he was eating. And in the other, he was shooting. And the screaming, it was unbearable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so as you were getting ready to lay down, a policeman tells you to run.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one says, don't lay down. People ran. And one looks at the other. We start already to go to lay down by the grave. And one looks at the other and one says, [NON-ENGLISH], let's run. So we started to run. And the bullets were one after another, but there were like 20 people, 10 and 10. 10 couples. And they were shooting one after another, the bullets after us. So I don't know how many killed, or how many what. I was the only one.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I sat on the tree day time. I went down and I ate the mushrooms. And I ate it like [NON-ENGLISH]-- like spinach during the day. My teeth were so black like-- like this-- from-- that I didn't have anything. OK. I think we're going to pause here, OK? This is the end of tape two in an interview with Edith Cimmer.
